frameworks.name: reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: value updates from reward prediction errors over multi-arm bandit choices

frameworks.name: normative analysis
frameworks.one_line: asks whether a known cognitive bias is reward-maximising under constraints

frameworks.name: bounded rationality
frameworks.one_line: decisions made with finite computational precision (decision noise)

core_question: Can a confirmatory bias in reinforcement-learning value updates be reward-maximising rather than suboptimal?
key_assumptions: value estimates update asymmetrically depending on whether the option was chosen and the sign of the reward prediction error
decision policy is corrupted by finite computational precision (choice noise)
multi-arm bandit simulations capture the relevant trade-offs of human RL
manipulations: magnitude and direction of the confirmatory update bias
level of decision noise corrupting choices
bandit task settings (range of experimental configurations)
key_findings: confirmatory biases yield higher cumulative reward than an unbiased update rule across a wide range of settings
advantage is largest when decisions are corrupted by noise
biased learners overestimate values of better bandits and underestimate values of worse bandits
interpretations: apparently suboptimal asymmetric learning is reward-maximising once finite decision precision is taken into account
value separation induced by the bias makes noisy choices more robust
